You'll walk away with things to try in your next class, whether it's a new course or a curricular refresh.
speakers: Rahul Bhargava, Northeastern University; Elisabeth Gawthrop, American Public Media; Alex Richards, Syracuse University; Matt Waite, University of Nebraska

title: Skills as executable tipsheets - a new way to collaborate on data projects with AI agents
description: Anthropic released Skills in October - a new mechanism for adding capabilities to AI agent tools such as Claude Code. Skills have a wide range of applications in data reporting. A skill is a Markdown file that explains to the coding agent how to achieve a task. It might explain how to connect to a database, or provide preferred patterns for building front-end components, or how to obtain and process US Census data, or common mistakes to avoid when reporting on jobs report. NICAR already has a strong culture of tipsheets. A skill is just a tipsheet with an AI agent as its intended audience! This session is good for those with some familiarity with coding agent tools, such as Claude Code. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Simon Willison, Datasette

title: Scraping without programming
description: "Scraping" is a catch-all word for grabbing information off a web page and into your spreadsheet - whether the website wants you to or not. This session will introduce easy, hands-on methods for scraping data from a live webpage without having to learn any code. We will use the ImportHTML and ImportXML formulas in Google Sheets. Scraping beginners welcome! Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Samantha Sunne, Independent journalist

title: Clustering and mapping text data for journalism
description: Analysis of large text datasets can be difficult, and finding story ideas can be even harder, especially when you're not sure what you're looking for. This session teaches you to explore large sets of documents using semantic maps-visual clusters with similar documents grouped together in a two-dimensional chart. Users will learn how to convert text documents into embeddings, visualize them in two dimensions, and explore the data for insights by applying filters by keyword or time period. Applications include: parsing FOIA responses, tracking similar legislative language, or finding new areas of concern in school board comments. Participants will leave with the ability to map and cluster a sample of text data with provided code. This session is good for those with some Python experience. Laptops will be provided. You will need a GitHub account to participate.
speakers: Aishi Chandra, Columbia University; Dhrumil Mehta, Tow Center at Columbia University; C.J. Robinson, Tow Center at Columbia University

title: From satellites to stories: Leveraging Google Earth Engine to analyze global environmental datasets for local reporting
description: Accessing openly published maps and models from the latest research has never been easier through platforms like Google Earth Engine (GEE), enabling journalists to expose systemic patterns of environmental harm at scale-including those not already captured in government statistics-while honing in on, based on data, the most salient case studies for detailed ground reporting. In this session, you will learn how to access the forest change dataset powering Global Forest Watch for free through GEE with just one line of code. You'll then quantify changes to forests in a local area and, most importantly, analyze this remotely sensed data together with detailed local data to illuminate impacts of environmental change on communities. This session is good for those with some experience working with geospatial data. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Carl Churchill, The Wall Street Journal

title: Investigating TikTok through data donations
description: The Post collaborated with 1,100 readers to create a unique dataset of TikTok watch histories -- which they used to investigate political content, the app's addictiveness, and how the algorithm favors certain kinds of content over others. They also published the first Map of TikTok that hasn't been published yet but will hopefully break the internet. We'll discuss this project from beginning to end, including participant recruitment, secure/private data processing, and technical hurdles to digging into a giant dataset.

title: Easy visualizations for your first data stories
description: Learn best practices for thinking about simple data visualizations to help tell your story - plus, a peek at some free tools!
speakers: Adrian D. Garcia, Financial Times Specialist; Haohsiang Ko, Financial Times; Prinz Magtulis, Reuters; Elena Mejia, Bloomberg

title: Extracting data from PDFs using command-line and other advanced tools
description: This class seeks to help you free data stored in PDFs. Attendees will extract text from a computer-generated PDF using command-line tools, process image files with optical character recognition and set themselves up for working with PDFs in Python. The session will also cover how to assess a PDF to select the right tools to use to free its data. This session is good for: People with experience using a command-line interface and who deal with frustrating PDFs. Knowledge of Python or R is a plus but not required. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Alex Garcia, Datasette

title: R 2: Data analysis and plotting (repeat)
description: We'll use the tidyverse packages dplyr and ggplot2, learning how to sort, filter, group, summarize, join, and visualize to identify trends in your data. If you want to combine SQL-like analysis and charting in a single pipeline, this session is for you. This session is good for: People who have worked with data operations in SQL or Excel and would like to do the same in R and have some experience working with RStudio. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Karina Kumar, Austin American-Statesman

title: Networking: Journalists of color sponsored by ProPublica
description: Mix and mingle, meet friends old and new, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for journalists of color.

title: Gemini for news
description: Level up your reporting with Gemini. This session focuses on key features like Deep Research for multi-step investigations, Custom Gems for scalable tasks, and Canvas for interactive drafting. Plus, discover how Gemini in Pinpoint enhances your research across large document sets.
speakers: Mago Torres, Google News Initiative

title: Using CAPTCHA-solving services to automate difficult web-scraping projects
description: This class will teach attendees how to use CAPTCHA-solving services to automate CAPTCHA solutions in web scraping. Attendees will learn how to efficiently identify and send required elements to the CAPTCHA-solving service and apply the unique codes received to navigate past CAPTCHAs encountered during web scraping. Special focus will be placed on tackling Google's reCAPTCHA v2, a commonly used CAPTCHA on the web, alongside strategies for overcoming challenges presented by more complex websites. The class will provide practical skills in CAPTCHA solving, equipping attendees with the knowledge to implement these solutions in their projects. This session is good for journalists with some Python experience. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Ryan Little, ProPublica

title: Investigating the Epstein Files
speakers: Rob Barry, The Wall Street Journal; Jack Gillum, The Wall Street Journal; Jaclyn Jeffrey-Wilensky, The Wall Street Journal; Caitlin Ostroff, The Wall Street Journal; Brian Whitton, The Wall Street Journal

title: Making better databases with Airtable
description: Airtable is a spreadsheet and database software with useful features to view, sort, group, and link records between tables. You'll see a few examples of how Airtable has been useful for the instructor's reporting (and why she almost never use Excel anymore!) and walk through a hands-on example. This session is good for: Anyone who has been frustrated at making multiple versions of the same spreadsheet, creating extra columns (or filters) and then repeatedly hiding and unhiding them, or jumping between multiple spreadsheets to bring together different information about the same record. This session is good for people with basic experience working with data. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets).
speakers: Amy Fan, The New York Times

title: Think spatially: Finding stories in geographic data with geopandas
description: Geographic data can do more than make pretty maps-it can help you find stories. In this hands-on session, you'll learn how to use Geopandas, a Python tool that lets you work with location data just like you work with spreadsheets. We'll focus on how to ask and answer reporting questions with geographic data: Which neighborhoods have the most potholes? Which schools are near industrial sites? Where do service gaps overlap? You'll learn how to combine and compare different datasets, look for patterns and boundaries, and measure what's happening where. We'll also look briefly at how to show your results on a map, but the main goal is to help you use location data for analysis-not just visualization. This session is good for people with experience working with Python. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Janie Haseman, Hearst

title: Modern document processing with Natural PDF
description: Writing Python scripts to extract data from PDFs is always a nightmare - misshapen tables, arbitrary form formatting, tiny text or low-quality scanned texts. Your champion is here in the form of Natural PDF, a new Python library for wrangling data that's focused on usability and cramming in as many features as possible. Write your code just like you'd ask "real-language" questions: - "Find the bold text that ends with a colon and get the text to the right of it" - "Show me all pages that include diagrams" - "Ignore everything above the thick horizontal line" Natural PDF also comes bundled with all sorts of modern AI-based magic: - Categorize pages or entire PDFs by text or vision models - Use powerful layout analysis engines to recognize images, tables, headers, and paragraphs - Multilingual OCR powered by multiple engines + LLM-correcting integration Participants will leave with a solid understanding of how to extract data from difficult (and simple) PDFs using Python. This session is good for those with some experience working with Python. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Jonathan Soma, Columbia University

title: Building web scrapers on the fly with ChatGPT and Claude
description: This session will focus on how to scrape data from public websites in breaking news and quick-turn situations. Use a tool like ChatGPT to help you quickly understand a website's structure and API calls to get data fast. This session is good for beginners. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Charles Minshew, Atlanta Journal-Constitution

title: Cheatsheet: An investigative spreadsheet tool powered by A.I.
description: In this session, we will demo Cheatsheet, an internal tool developed at The New York Times that has helped in the reporting process for dozens of breaking news and investigative stories. Cheatsheet is a smart spreadsheet application that makes it quick and easy to analyze large datasets, document dumps and media collections at scale using A.I. systems. Cheatsheet gives reporters the ability to quickly find needles in large haystacks using recipes such as A.I. classification, translation, summarization, quote extraction and web search.
speakers: Dylan Freedman, The New York Times; Teresa Mondría Terol, The New York Times

title: Advanced visualization and design in Datawrapper
description: Take your Datawrapper skills to the next level. This session will cover advanced features and techniques to make your visualizations more powerful, polished and interactive. Learn how to customize design themes and use advanced chart types to communicate complex data clearly and creatively. Some basic HTML knowledge is preferred, but not necessary. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Jovi Dai, National Association of REALTORS®

title: How to do data-driven work in small communities
description: The Data-Driven Reporting Project was founded to support newsrooms who might otherwise lack the tools, training or capacity to do more technically-focused investigative reporting. These are often smaller newsrooms with fewer resources. This in-person panel discussion at NICAR will show examples of how journalists, who have received support from the Data-Driven Reporting Project, are navigating complex and data-driven stories on small and underserved communities. Speakers will share stories and how they problem-solved connecting with the people behind the data. Good for beginners.
speakers: Jill Blackman, Northwestern University; Pam Dempsey, Northwestern University; Chris Farone, Boston Institute for Nonprofit Journalism; Erica Peterson, Mountain State Spotlight; Aallyah Wright, Capital B News

title: R 3: Gathering and cleaning data (repeat)
description: Learn how to import a wide variety of files in R including spreadsheets, files on the web and HTML tables, and transform the results into usable data. This session will also focus on how to clean and structure the data you've gathered in preparation for analysis using tidyverse packages. This session is good for: People who have some experience using R and the Tidyverse. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Katherine Oung, Houston Chronicle

title: Behind the stories: 2025 Philip Meyer Winners
description: A data deep dive into the 2025 Philip Meyer Award winners. Hear from reporters on how they gathered, cleaned, analyzed and visualized the data behind some of the year's biggest stories.
speakers: Brant Houston, University of Illinois

title: Finding the story: Transportation data
description: This session will empower you to use data from the National Transportation Safety Board to investigative major railway accidents and overlooked safety recommendations. You'll gain practical skills to identify your own local stories on railway safety. Some spreadsheets experience will be useful for this session. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Shradha Dinesh, McClatchy; Cat Murphy, University of Maryland

title: The art of the data FOIA
description: In this session, learn how to get exactly what you want from government agencies, in the format you want it in, and with all the fields you want. This hands-on session is great for beginners and anyone who wants to polish their FOIA skills. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.

title: The art of networking - and why it's vital
description: What networking isn't: Collecting business cards and letting them gather dust in your desk drawer. What netowrking is: A way to build two-way relationships that turn into long-term connections. This workshop offers tips on creating the perfect elevator pitch to introduce yourself, finding the right people to meet and starting to build mutually beneficial conversations that translate into opportunities, collaborators and purposeful long-term connections.
speakers: Benét Wilson, IRE & NICAR

title: 2025 Philip Meyer Awards presentation
Lightning Talks ⚡️
description: Sometimes you don't need 45 minutes to explain a useful technique or interesting resource. Join your colleagues for a session of short (5-minute) talks about doing data journalism, web development and related topics.
speakers: Adam Rhodes, IRE & NICAR

title: NIghtCAR (game night) ✨
description: Join fellow NICAR attendees for an evening of games, puzzles, etc. IRE provides the board games, you provide the fun! Feel free to grab a snack and a beverage from the reception or from the hotel and join the fun.

title: Philip Meyer Awards reception
description: Celebrate the Philip Meyer Award winners at a reception to honor them. Light appetizers and a drink ticket will be provided.

title: The price of recovery: How to investigate opioid settlement spending
description: As states, counties, cities, and tribes begin spending more than $80 billion in opioid settlement funds, questions of transparency and accountability are increasingly urgent. In this session, five graduate students from West Virginia University's Reed School of Media will share how they used investigative and data reporting techniques to track settlement spending in their state - and how this approach can be replicated in your communities! Panelists will outline key strategies for data collection, open records requests, and newsroom partnerships, while reflecting on journalism's role in holding institutions accountable and amplifying community voices in responses to the opioid crisis.
speakers: Hannah Heiskell, West Virginia University; Claudi Di Lima, West Virginia University; Ty McClung, West Virginia University; Jessica Riley, West Virginia University; Drew Solt, West Virginia University

title: Auditing AI algorithms for bias (and sometimes using AI tools to do so)
description: AI is everywhere and reporters are finding new ways to hold AI tools accountable, sometimes using AI to do so. This panel will discuss how journalists can identify bias in predictive and generative AI tools built by some of the largest technology companies, plus speakers will share which AI tools they use for investigations and research.
speakers: Sean Mussenden, University of Maryland; Hilke Schellmann, Independent journalist; Daniel Trielli, University of Maryland; Lucy Zimmerman, Stanford University

title: How "Skills" solve the biggest pitfall in using AI for data analysis in Claude Code & OpenAI Codex
description: We've all been there recently. Jazzed at experimenting with the latest AI/LLM tooling to help drive our data journalism. But there remains a particularly thorny issue when using AI tools for data analysis: they don't do things the same way twice, they can go rogue, and they're awful at math. They may be great at building web apps, but they can be very dangerous for doing actual data analysis. But the "skills" workflow has the potential to be a game-changer. They began in Claude Code and have now been adopted by Codex as well. What are "skills"...? They allow you to create specific, reproducible code chunks or even entire scripts that will then get executed. The file system is dead-simple: just markdown files and your code scripts. And from there, skills functions largely as an automation tool - it'll run what you've told it to run, when you tell it to, with certain variables provided by you or your colleagues. And it will ensure Claude or Codex gives you consistent, reproducible results every time. Learn what it's all about. This session is good for people with some coding experience. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Aaron Kessler, Associated Press

title: Data team starter kit: How to build a data team from the ground up
description: How do you set a new data team up for success? How do you grow a data team while maintaining your team's identity and standards? This session will tackle best practices for establishing and maintaining standards on new and growing data teams. We'll cover automating onboarding, standardizing project structure with Datakit and setting up a robust fact-checking process.
speakers: Sahana Jayaraman, The Baltimore Banner; Greg Morton, The Baltimore Banner; Allan James Vestal, The Baltimore Banner

title: Data collection at scale for countries where data is hidden
description: Learn about collecting data for countries where data is difficult to obtain, hidden, or behind wonky websites that don't give you access to actual insights. Find out how to obtain data and support reporting pipelines - also how to gather data that's more qualitative to supplement the numbers.
speakers: Alexander Borodikhin, Mediazona; Tazbia Fatima, Hearst; Inaara Gangji, UC Berkeley; Eunjee Wi, Arizona State University

title: Beyond the big leagues: Data journalism in high school sports
description: High school sports drive huge traffic but lack the data infrastructure of pro leagues. This demo will explore how journalists build databases and tell data-driven stories when official stats are incomplete or nonexistent. Panelists will share strategies for scraping and standardizing local sports data, creating searchable tools for communities, and making compelling visualizations. Attendees will learn how to find stories in oft-incomplete sports data and build resources that serve coaches, parents, and fans beyond a single season.
speakers: Rahul Deshpande, Atlanta Journal-Constitution; Josh Fine, HBO

title: Using regular expressions
description: By the end of this class, this expression -- ^[A-Za-z0-9._-]+@[[A-Za-z0-9.-]+$ -- will make sense to you! You'll learn how to construct advanced searches on text data using regular expressions, a powerful pattern-matching syntax that can help you find needles in haystacks. This session is good for those with some basic experience working with data, and anyone who wants to learn to use regular expressions. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Ethan Corey, Behind Bars Data Project

title: Google Sheets: Advanced pivot tables
description: You've done a few pivot tables and are getting curious about what more you could do with them. What happens if you aggregate by more than one column? What are those "column" and "filter" boxes for? Come unlock the full potential of pivot tables in this intermediate spreadsheet class. This session is good for: People familiar with spreadsheets and aggregating data with pivot tables, or anyone who has taken Sheets 1-3. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) for the training and will need a free Google account to participate.
speakers: Koby Levin, Outlier Media

title: Using LLMs to improve your archives
description: Most newsroom archives contain years of institutional knowledge unread by most. This session shows how to unlock them to make your reporters more informed and to help onboard new journalists. Drawing on a university course where journalism students with little coding experience built "beat books" from local newspaper archives from Maryland's Eastern Shore, we'll walk participants through a reproducible pipeline using open and commercial LLMs. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Clayton Ludwig, University of Maryland; Cat Murphy, University of Maryland; Derek Willis, University of Maryland

title: Introduction to R
description: We'll introduce you to R, a free, powerful open-source programming language that will take your data reporting to the next level. By the end of this three-hour session, you will be able to read data from common file types into R, clean and explore it, create visualizations, and make your entire data workflow reproducible. We'll also talk about how to find help when you're stuck. Workshop prerequisites: This session will be most helpful if you're comfortable working with data and you're ready to take your skills to the next level. Preregistration is required and seating is limited. Laptops will be provided for the training.
speakers: Liz Lucas, University of Missouri; Mariia Novoselia, University of Missouri

title: Media lawyer Q&A: Student edition
speakers: Kris Cundiff, Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (RCFP); Eric Feder, Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (RCFP); Maggie Mulvihill, Boston University

title: Find public records and leaks: The new OCCRP Aleph Pro
description: You've got a leak, a name, or a suspicious company - what's your next move? In a world of complex financial secrecy, uncovering the truth demands data power. Join this hands-on workshop to master the all-new OCCRP Aleph Pro, holding over four billion records of leaks, sanctions, corporate registries, and beyond. For years, Aleph has been the backbone of hundreds of investigations, and we have now evolved it to meet the challenges of modern reporting. Bring your laptop and learn to cross-reference targets, generate structured entities from raw text, and map hidden connections into visual networks. You will also learn how to secure access to protected leaks curated for journalists, activists, and researchers. It's time to power your next investigation using Aleph Pro. Please fill out this form by Wednesday, March 4th, to have an account created and access granted for OCCRP Aleph Pro. Because this will be a highly interactive session where you'll have the opportunity to create your own investigations and visual networks using Aleph Pro, prior account setup is strictly required. This session is good for beginners and anyone who wants to learn to use Aleph Pro.
speakers: Ezana Ćeman, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project; Klil Eden, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project

title: Finding the story: Using data to report on statehouses
description: Whenever journalists are told to follow the money, we know that always means having a strong grasp on the data that is used to advocate for bills and the backdrop of who may be funding what. Come learn how to dig into the data out there with some hands-on exercises. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Marina Villeneuve, Hechinger Report

title: Elevate your graphics with Illustrator!
description: Illustrator is a great tool for creating beautiful, completely unique data viz and graphics - but the best part is it doesn't need to all be done from scratch. In this session, we will go over ways to begin making your chart using various online tools that most newsrooms already use (like Datawrapper, RawGraphs, Flourish, ggplot, etc.) but elevate its design with the magic of SVGs and vector graphics programs like Adobe Illustrator. We'll talk through color choices, fonts, accessibility, framing, and more - and give you tips and tricks to create stunning graphics that you can customize to your heart's delight. This session is good for beginners. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Paroma Soni, POLITICO; Stephanie Stamm, The Wall Street Journal

title: Using open-source intelligence (OSINT) in investigations
description: This session will show how journalists can begin incorporating open-source research methodologies in their investigations, with examples from real articles.
speakers: Gabriel Geiger, Lighthouse Reports; Jennefer Harper, Decoherence Media; Alison Killing, Financial Times; Tristan Lee, Decoherence Media

title: Scraping dynamic websites with browser automation tools
description: Playwright is a next-generation browser automation tool that allows you to use Python or JavaScript to scrape almost any web page. It can assist in downloading pages of government documents, capturing tweets before they get deleted, or even just breaking past the cookie consent banner. Beyond the basics, it can also easily take screenshots, monitor and log network requests, and even fit right into your traditional BeautifulSoup scraping approach. We'll look at: - Installing Playwright - Accessing elements on the page - Interacting with web pages (clicking, navigating, filling out forms) - Taking screenshots - Sending pages to traditional scraping tools like BeautifulSoup - Common patterns including pagination and CAPTCHA breaking For those of you familiar with tackling similar problems using Selenium: Playwright is a similar tool with a better interface, better install/upgrade process, and ten times the usability. It might be time to upgrade! Participants should have a basic knowledge of Python and HTML, but we'll also cover how to breeze past those basics with AI assistance. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Jonathan Soma, Columbia University

title: Becoming acquainted with your city as a data reporter with no beat
description: Early-career, local data journalists might find themselves needing to quickly navigate the data ecosystem of a new place for the first time. In this session, panelists will share their tips and tricks for: - Learning what kinds of data are easily available in your new home - Building out a "beat" for yourself - Collaborating across multiple desks - working with a wide array of datasets
speakers: Sahana Jayaraman, The Baltimore Banner; Kara Newhouse, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism; Bianca Pallaro, The New York Times; Elliott Ramos, CBS News

title: The winding paths of a data journalist
description: Data journalists have unique skills and experiences that are applicable to product design and management roles. Learn from panelists who have navigated unexpected journeys to find their niche in the data world. This panel is great for people who are curious about making a career shift, and hearing from professionals who have firsthand experience in making these transitions. Our panelists will share their strategies and lessons learned, offering valuable insights for those considering a similar career pivot.
speakers: Allan Lasser, MuckRock; Arlene Martinez, Good Jobs First; Elizabeth Renter, NerdWallet; Tejal Wakchoure, NBC

title: Reporting on disappearing data about LGBTQ+ people
description: Reporters all over the country have scrambled to find and use quality data after the federal government deleted thousands of records about DEI, health, and the environment, to name a few. But as LGBTQ+ people are a notable target of the Trump Administration, it's not surprising that data about LGBTQ+ people has gone dark, been altered or is otherwise now more difficult to find. Come to this session to learn about what has happened to data about LGBTQ+ people, where to find it, and what happens when you need to be your own data architect.
speakers: Zak Cassel, WFYI; Adam Rhodes, IRE & NICAR; Emilia Ruzicka, Independent journalist

title: Deep dive: Navigating nonprofit data
description: More than half a million organizations - from your local humane society to some of the largest hospital chains in the nation - file annual public tax returns detailing revenue, expenses, staffing information, and more. In this hands-on session, we'll use spreadsheets to dig into filing data and answer questions about nonprofit organizations that you can apply to nearly any beat or coverage area. No coding experience is necessary, but we'll discuss ways to dig further into these datasets using code. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate.
speakers: Ellis Simani, ProPublica; Andrea Suozzo, ProPublica

title: Networking: Women in data
description: In this session, women, trans and non-binary folks in data journalism and investigative reporting will discuss the unique challenges they face, including the nuances of working in the field, issues of imposter syndrome and how to be our own advocates. Participants will have a safe space to ask questions, share experiences and network with one another.
speakers: Maggie Green, ABC News

title: First PMTiles Map: How to map massive datasets
description: Join Ben Welsh and Jon Schleuss to learn how to display a massive dataset on an interactive map using PMTiles and MapLibre. This one-hour, hands-on class will show you how to: Convert massive shapefiles to the compact PMTiles format using tippecanoe Display the tiles on an interactive map using MapLibre GL JS Create a data-driven style that colors features based on their attributes
speakers: Jon Schleuss, NewsGuild; Ben Welsh, Reuters

title: Big Local News tools, wares and resources
description: A lightning-round showcase of innovative newsroom tools and other resources that can help you collaborate on data journalism projects, harvest data and documents at scale, and track institutions for accountability reporting, from police agencies to state legislatures.
speakers: Rosie Cima, Big Local News; Cheryl Phillips, Big Local News; Ryan Pitts, Big Local News; Gerald Rich, Big Local News; Serdar Tumgoren, Big Local News

title: From spreadsheet to map in 60 minutes
description: Have a spreadsheet with latitude and longitude coordinates and need to turn it into a map quickly? This hands-on QGIS session focuses on the practical workflow journalists use most: importing tabular data, creating a point layer, adding geographic context, and styling a clear thematic map ready for publication. We'll skip the broad GIS overview and dive straight into the essential steps so you leave with a publishable map and a repeatable workflow you can use in your reporting.
speakers: Jennifer LaFleur, UC Berkeley School of Journalism; Charles Minshew, Atlanta Journal-Constitution

title: How to get a pile of documents processed for machine learning
speakers: Rob Wells, University of Maryland

title: Office Hours: one-on-ones with managers
description: Looking for specific feedback on a cover letter or portfolio piece? Maybe you want to wordsmith a section of your resume or update your bio. Sign up for a 20-min one-on-one session with a manager or newsroom leader with expertise in data, editing, investigations, career pathways and more

title: Immigration data and how to make sense of it
description: The Trump administration has drastically reshaped immigration enforcement. Newsrooms around the country are trying to make sense of what is happening to their communities. Join this panel to learn how reporters break down the most important immigration data sets and how that data can be used to tell both local and national stories.
speakers: Will Craft, The Guardian; Elena Mejia, Bloomberg; Tanvi Misra, Independent journalist; Leon Yin, Bloomberg News

title: How to educate journalism students about AI and with AI
description: Artificial intelligence is here to stay, so it's important to have a plan in place to educate the next generation of journalists. This panel of journalists and educators will hold a town hall-style session to share strategies and ideas on effective ways to educate journalism students about AI. Attendees will learn about what has and hasn't worked, how to use and build tools and how to be ethical and accountable when using AI.
speakers: Hilke Schellmann, Independent journalist

title: Automating your workflows with Github Actions
description: There are a ton of routine data sets that we pull regularly to analyze. Crime, housing, construction. But what if you could do that while you sleep? Github Actions allows you to do just that. Participants should have a basic knowledge of Github. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Sean McMinn, POLITICO

title: Presents for your future self: Automated workflows for making charts and maps
description: When you're working on a project with shifting or updating data, there's nothing better you can do for yourself than to build your own little sprocket bot. In this workshop, learn how you can build that bot through command line tools (makefile, node.js, python) to automate tasks like mapmaking. Learn workflow tips for Illustrator, Photoshop, Blender, and more! Save time by setting up a file and forever more executing it via a push of the button. Folks with an understanding of command line tools will get the most out of this session. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Dylan Moriarty, The Washington Post

title: Beyond the investigation: Managing stress, safety & mental wellness
description: NO RECORDING! Covering sensitive topics can take an emotional toll, including long working hours, emotional strain and personal sacrifices. A panel of journalists will share strategies that help attendees recognize early signs of stress, create grounding techniques and find healthy outlets outside of work to protect their mental health.
speakers: Cate Charron, Indy Star; Lakeidra Chavis, The Marshall Project; Disha Raychaudhuri, Reuters

title: Data crowd competition: 40 ideas in 40 minutes
description: This session invites attendees to compete for prizes for offering the coolest, most unique public datasets that every journalist should request. Each person gets one minute to come up to the mic and explain their dataset. The idea will be entered into a list of the screen, and then at the end attendees vote for their favorite. The top three win fabulous IRE merchandise.
speakers: David Cuillier, Brechner FOI Project; Cierra Putman, WTHR; Samantha Sunne, Independent journalist

title: Covering growing corporate power, even on deadline
description: As corporate influence and power grows, it grows increasingly important to document the broader impact of business leaders' actions. For instance, companies are fined billions of dollars each year for stealing workers' wages, degrading the environment, defrauding the government, cheating consumers, and other misconduct - using Violation Tracker, reporters can look up the behavior of companies across more than 450 federal, state, and local regulatory agencies. Many of those same companies also receive billions in taxpayer subsidies in the name of economic development - using Subsidy Tracker, reporters can explore those tax breaks and other giveaways. Taken together, these two databases can help a reporter find patterns and trends in the ways businesses operate, and explore the ways governments enable poor behavior by corporate leaders to continue.
speakers: Arlene Martinez, Good Jobs First

title: How to avoid a midterm meltdown: Tips for covering elections
description: This panel will show how journalists navigate covering elections with data and how to avoid common mistakes. Speakers will share tips and stories about reporting on election results, campaign finance, and voter turnout.
speakers: Alexandra Glorioso, Miami Herald; Ence Morse, The Washington Post; Phoebe Quinton, Atlanta Journal-Constitution; Derek Willis, University of Maryland

title: Using Python for data analysis
description: Imagine rolling Excel and SQL into one tool that also allows you to track your code and share it: That's pandas in a nutshell. There's a lot more you can do with it, of course, but this will be a good start. We'll learn how to slice and dice our data and extract basic stats. Specifically, we'll cover loading the data, filtering, sorting and grouping data. This class is good for: People who are comfortable with Excel and are familiar with the basics of SQL and Python. We recommend that you attend a Python 101 session or have equivalent experience before coming to this class. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Caitlin Ostroff, The Wall Street Journal

title: Google Sheets: Importing & data prep
description: Don't give up if your data isn't presented in a neat spreadsheet. This session will teach you how to get data into a spreadsheet and prepare it for analysis. We will look at how to import text files, deal with data in a PDF, and get a table on a web page into a spreadsheet. This session is good for: Anyone comfortable working in Google Sheets. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) for the training and will need a free Google account to participate.
speakers: Rosmery Izaguirre, POLITICO

title: Excel 2: Formulas & sorting (Mac lab)
description: Much of Excel's power comes in the form of formulas. In this class, you'll learn how to use them to analyze data with the eye of a journalist. Yes, math will be involved, but it's totally worth it! This class will show you how calculations like change, percent change, rates and ratios can beef up your reporting. This class will be taught on Mac computers. This session is good for: Anyone who is comfortable navigating Excel. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Jessica Huseman, Votebeat

title: Lunch bunch
description: Make some new friends over lunch! Show up and we'll connect you with other folks who want to chat and network over the lunch break -- a great way to make a big conference feel smaller. Meet at the bottom of the escalators on Level 1.

title: Collaborating in an intergenerational workspace: let's get to yes
description: From Boomers to Alphas, there is currently a wider range of ages than ever trying to create extraordinary journalism side-by-side during ever-changing and unprecedented times. Technology has created levels of haves and have-nots, digital natives and cultural luddites. But at the end of the day, our teams are here to serve our audiences and our communities: together. Let's talk about ways we can collaborate and find common ground, even when it feels sticky or difficult. Participants will come away with a framework for collaborative decision-making and have the chance to roleplay in groups, trying different mock scenarios among peers.
speakers: Emma Carew Grovum, Kimbap Media; Will Lager, Independent journalist; Garry Pierre-Pierre, URL Media; Teresa Mondría Terol, The New York Times

title: Fun with hypotheticals: Investigating your dubious data dilemmas in real time
description: From the people who dared to ask "How many rats are in the Rainforest Cafe?" at NICAR25 comes an interactive panel on approaching a data story with an investigative mindset - this year done in real time. Attendees are encouraged to submit their most ridiculous investigative questions ahead of and during the panel. As a group, we'll take a few of those questions and propose and refine a hypothesis, make a reporting plan, brainstorm records requests, and more. By the end of the session, participants will be equipped to craft an investigative data story from scratch - starting from even the most unexpected or whimsical question. Submit your ideas at https://tinyurl.com/hypotheticals26.
speakers: Adiel Kaplan, Tow-Knight Center for Journalism Futures; Veronica Penney, American Association for the Advancement of Science; Emily Zentner, The California Newsroom

title: How to use AI responsibly
description: Journalists across the world are trying to determine how and when to use GenAI and other machine learning techniques for reporting. Panelists from a range of projects and newsrooms will discuss how they considered journalistic ethics while using AI. Attendees should leave with ideas on when to combine AI classification with manual review, what to disclose to audiences and other practical tips.
speakers: Dylan Freedman, The New York Times; Sachita Nishal, Northwestern University; Lisa Pickoff-White, California Reporting Project; Katey Rusch, UC Berkeley

title: Using coding agents for data analysis
description: Coding agents such as Claude Code and OpenAI Codex are mainly marketed at developers, but they're actually applicable to a much wider array of problems, including data analysis, data cleaning, web scraping and other tasks commonly faced by data journalists. In this session, you'll learn how to apply these tools to both simple and ambitious data reporting projects. Attendees will go away with a very powerful new tool for collecting, exploring, analyzing and presenting data. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Simon Willison, Datasette

title: Building your first SvelteKit data app
description: SvelteKit is a user-friendly JavaScript framework that allows you to easily build and deploy web apps. Join us for a crash course in using SvelteKit to make a data-driven interactive. This session is great for those with frontend development knowledge. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Tyler Machado, Independent journalist

title: Rubric-a-palooza
description: At this demo party, we'll discuss the Science of Teaching and Learning (SoTL) behind effective ways to give students feedback. Ideally, we'll learn tips and tricks from one another as well as from the literature and do a hands-on exercise using AI tools to help us write effective rubrics for our assignments.
speakers: Dhrumil Mehta, Tow Center at Columbia University; Aarushi Sahejpal, American University

title: Scraping with Python
description: In this workshop you'll learn how to investigate websites, evaluate scraping difficulty and use Python to scrape data not otherwise easily obtained with out-of-the-box tools. Among the examples you'll discuss is a project to scrape some difficult data out of the Florida Financial System. Preregistration is required and seating is limited. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to this training.
speakers: Aïcha Camara, McClatchy; Alexandra Glorioso, Miami Herald

title: Elections 2026: Uncovering influence operations in the U.S.
description: This panel will demonstrate how to use tools to investigate and track influence operations. Journalists will learn how to piece together opaque spending networks and uncover crucial details about operations through tax returns, Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA) filings, digital ad data, corporate records, lobbying disclosures and other sources.
speakers: Anna Massoglia, MuckRock

title: Making public data actually public: Building tools to serve communities
description: Public records are technically "public," but that doesn't mean they're accessible. This panel features journalists who've taken hard-to-use government data and transformed it into searchable databases that serve their communities. Panelists will share case studies of look up tools for stories like city council redistricting, voter registration cancellation and more. We'll discuss how to navigate the technical and editorial decisions involved in cleaning and publishing messy government data, the reality of maintaining these tools long-term, and maintaining reader/user/data privacy.
speakers: Rahul Deshpande, Atlanta Journal-Constitution; Jennifer LaFleur, UC Berkeley School of Journalism; Cam Rodriguez, Independent journalist; Kevin Uhrmacher, ProPublica

title: Custom dataviz with JavaScript 101
description: Interested in building data visualizations for the web? This session will introduce you to charts made with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, helping you understand how various components combine to create a complete visualization displayed in your browser by coding. This session is good for journalists who are new to HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Andrew Briz, POLITICO

title: Extracting data from PDFs using off-the-shelf tools
description: Join this class to learn how to liberate data trapped inside PDF files. This class will cover basic approaches for getting text out of PDF documents using powerful and freely available tools. Participants will be introduced to basic concepts and walked through tackling common challenges encountered with tricky PDF documents. This session is good for beginners. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Maggie Mulvihill, Boston University

title: Spatial analysis in R: 101
description: Learn how to use spatial functions in R to level up your reporting. We'll explore tools for mapping, spatial joins, buffering and calculating distance - all using a single package. We'll center a story published by The Baltimore Banner on gun violence around schools to showcase an application of these functions and demonstrate best practices for basic geospatial analysis. This session is good for beginner and intermediate journalists with some familiarity with R. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Ryan Little, ProPublica; Nick McMillan, ProPublica

title: Cracking the code: How to read local government budgets
description: Local government budgets are plans - Hopes. Dreams. Needs. Priorities. They reflect power. They make promises. In this session, you'll learn how to read them and how to find the enterprise stories in the numbers. I'll also share takeaways from public finance professionals, including how they navigate the politics in the budgeting process. Lessons in this session apply to all local council, commission, school and authority budgets. This session is good for all levels of experience! Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.
speakers: AmyJo Brown, War Streets Media

title: How to categorize and clean text data with keywords, LLMs and embeddings: A roundtable
description: Our datasets are often not just numbers but free-text descriptions -- AI chatbot logs, TikTok transcripts or government contract purposes. Three-ish reporters discuss how to categorize this kind of text data with three very different techniques: ol' fashioned keywords, embeddings and LLMs. We'll discuss the trade-offs between cost, precision and recall and interpretability -- and of course tell some war stories. We'll also discuss how to come up with taxonomies that make sense and how to refine each technique.
speakers: Jeremy Merrill, The Washington Post; Andrea Suozzo, ProPublica; Brian Whitton, The Wall Street Journal

title: Continuous improvement for reporters: Kaizen, preflight checklists and more
description: Let's talk about how we can make reviewing and improving our work and our recurring processes (both automated and manual) a core part of our workflow as reporters and editors. Panelists will discuss goal setting, review and improvement processes like the "plan act do check" cycle, the broader philosophy of growth through incremental change and documentation, and how systems used in manufacturing, aviation, tech and other industries can be adapted for reporters, especially when it comes to data and investigative work. This won't just be hypothetical: we'll discuss practical ways to turn these systems and philosophies into real-world workflows for both freelance and staff journalists.
speakers: Audrey Nielsen, MuckRock

title: "Vibe-coding" for experimentation and deadlines
description: Picture this: There's a data presentation format you've wanted to try for ages. Now, all of a sudden, you've got a perfect story to use it on - but it's running in two days. Your current systems can't make it happen. Third-party solutions don't fit your organization's look and feel. Starting from a blank code-editor window wouldn't get it done in time. What to do? This session will show how one journalist solved that problem, using agentic AI to describe an intended solution - and then iterate on functionality - all while guarding against AI hallucinations and other possible pitfalls. It's not a silver bullet, and it won't absolve you of having to vet logic and think critically. But when done right, it can be a massive force multiplier for someone who has the technical knowledge (but needs to meet a tight deadline). Folks with some coding knowledge will get the most out of this session. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Allan James Vestal, The Baltimore Banner

title: To whom it may concern: An honest conversation with data team hiring managers
description: It's hard to know what to expect when interviewing for data journalism jobs. What are hiring managers looking for in your resume and cover letter? Are you expected to have a personal website, Github or social media presence? What should you come prepared to talk about during each stage of the interview process? Join us for a panel discussion on how different news organizations approach hiring. Be sure to bring your questions for the Q&A segment. Please note that this session will not be recorded.
speakers: Will Craft, The Guardian; Hannah Fresques, ProPublica; Caroline Ghisolfi, Houston Chronicle; Meghan Hoyer, The Washington Post; Allie Kanik, Houston Chronicle

title: Turn the impossible into the doable: The random sample as a reporting tool
description: You want to know how often people get their wages garnished because of unpaid medical bills...but it would take years to comb through all those court cases. Or maybe you have a long list of people deemed fraudsters and want to know how many of them are still operating businesses...but it would be impractical to look up each one. It's time for a random sample. This session addresses how to use random sampling as a reporting tool, and how to write up your findings for a lay audience. We'll review questions like: How big of a sample do you need? How can you design your random sample so that certain populations or geographies (like rural areas) don't get ignored?
speakers: Rae Ellen Bichell, KFF Health News; Maia Rosenfeld, KFF Health News

title: Custom dataviz with JavaScript 102
description: Interested in building data visualizations for the web? This session will continue the exploration in the 101 session of various charts made with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, helping you understand how various components combine to create a complete visualization displayed in your browser by coding. This session is good for journalists with a bit of HTML and CSS experience, or participants who already attended "Custom dataviz with JavaScript 101". Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Andrew Williams, San Francisco Chronicle

title: Spatial analysis in R: 201
description: Take your spatial skills up a notch by learning to use image-based data derived from satellite observations. In this session, you'll discover how to navigate tricky filetypes like netCDF and TIF, all within the comfort of RStudio. Learn how to extract decades of real-time measurements from variables like ocean heat, temperature, land use, air pollution and more. By the end, you'll have the tools to join satellite measurements with traditional shapefiles. This session is good for journalists who are comfortable with the basics of R and Rstudio. Attending "Spatial analysis in R: 101" is recommended. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Ryan Little, ProPublica; Nick McMillan, ProPublica

title: ezesri: Mining public mapping data from Esri services
description: Local governments often publish spatial data to mapping giant Esri's platforms before posting it anywhere else. Learn how to use ezesri to discover more than 100,000 public data sets and extract them without code in journalist-friendly formats (csv, geoJSON, shapefile). The tool also features a Python package for power users. Some data experience will be useful for this session. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Matt Stiles, CNN

title: ICE enforcement violence needs a collaborative database!
description: Several newsrooms are collecting and reporting on instances of immigration enforcement violence. The Trace, The Marshall Project and The New York Times and others have at different points this year chronicled the violence of federal immigration agents across the U.S. But this moment calls for a comprehensive and collective effort -- not a competitive one. Join brains to plan and execute a national reporting database that will catalog as many instances of immigration enforcement violence as possible. Together we'll develop a plan, roles, and tooling to begin broad collaborative tracking of immigration agent violence. We'll name the project and draft a pitch document for news nerds to bring back to their senior managers for official organization-level adoption and sponsoring. Past projects like this have done nothing short of shape history -- COVID Tracking Project, anybody?? Come help make the most of these painful times.
speakers: George LeVines, The Trace

title: Excel 3: Filtering & pivot tables (Mac lab)
description: A look at the awesome power of pivot - and how to use it to analyze your dataset in minutes rather than hours. We'll work up to using a pivot table by first sorting and filtering a dataset, learning how to find story ideas along the way. This class will be taught on Mac computers. This session is good for: Anyone familiar with formulas, sorting and filtering in Excel or another spreadsheet program. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Tyler Dukes, McClatchy

title: Digging into death with CDC Wonder
description: CDC Wonder contains a treasure trove of public health information based on death certificates across the country. This session will provide a quick start guide on using the website, pitfalls to avoid and ideas for stories you can take home -- like maternal mortality, overdose and cancer clusters. This session is good for beginners. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Destiny Herbers, Flatwater Free Press

title: Blockchain sleuthing 101
description: This hands-on session will teach you how to explore blockchain data to find stories, from general transactions to betting markets like Polymarket. This session is good for beginners. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Sam Kessler, The Wall Street Journal; Caitlin Ostroff, The Wall Street Journal

title: Beyond ironclad: Fact-checking the data story
description: Even with best practices, the most meticulous data reporter in the world can make mistakes. Catching these errors before publication can involve hours of tedious code review and debugging. Missing them can lead to corrections, retractions, or worse. This session will give attendees a hands-on demonstration of a data story fact-check. Participants will learn how to spot errors in data interpretation, how to catch and debug coding errors, and how to fix mistakes efficiently. We'll walk through an example data analysis containing errors based on real mistakes made by award-winning data journalists. (All examples will be fully anonymized!) Some sample errors: - Number parsing fails due to commas as the thousands separator - Invalid demographic analyses based on misinterpreted Census data - Dropped rows in joined datasets (inner joins considered harmful!) - Unweighted aggregations of percentages and other fractions This session is good for those with some coding experience. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Ethan Corey, Behind Bars Data Project

title: Uncovering wrongdoing with AI: Real-world methods and ethical boundaries
description: In this panel, journalists who have deployed AI on cross-border investigations share practical techniques, limits and guardrails for using LLMs and machine learning in data reporting. Case studies include: scraping court records and classifying gender-based violence cases in Eswatini using GPT-assisted workflows; using a hybrid of OCR, regular expressions and LLMs to classify and review tens of thousands of environmental impact documents in France; and using machine-learning tools to detect deforestation and forest fraud across Southeast Asia by building on existing scientific research. Sharing reproducible workflows, panellists will show how to combine human judgment and feedback with machine assistance for document processing, pattern detection, classification and verification. The discussion will focus on human-in-the-loop systems, responsible disclosure of AI-assisted work and strategies for managing large document sets without compromising editorial credibility and scepticism. Attendees will leave with concrete methods for turning messy, unstructured data into searchable investigative resources, practical verification checklists and guidance on how to document and explain AI-assisted reporting to editors and audiences.
speakers: Eva Constantaras, Lighthouse Reports; Sanjin Ibrahimovic, MuckRock; Sotirios Sideris, Nieman Foundation for Journalism at Harvard University; Jonathan Soma, Columbia University

title: Code Buddies: Get help on your data project
description: Need help on a data project? Having trouble figuring out how to use that cool tool you learned about in another session? Just want to network with some other news nerds? Stop by this session and get one-on-one help from experienced data journalists who would be delighted to help you solve your problems.
speakers: Jovi Dai, National Association of REALTORS®; Holly Hacker, KFF Health News

title: Scraping without programming (repeat)
description: Yes, you can scrape data without using code -- in fact, all you need is Google Sheets! We'll be using Excel-type formulas (don't worry if you don't know what those are, either) to make simple scrapers that automatically pull data into Google Sheets. It's the best way to get around clunky websites and unhelpful PIOs! This session is good for: Beginners who want to start using data for their stories. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) for the training.
speakers: Samantha Sunne, Independent journalist

title: The Data Journalism Podcast LIVE! with ProPublica
description: Scott Klein and Simon Rogers of the Data Journalism Podcast sit down with the team behind ProPublica's Meyer Award-winning series, "Life of a Mother." Reporter Andrea Suozzo and Deputy Data Editor Hannah Fresques go behind-the-scenes on their investigation into preventable deaths after the Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade. Plus, we'll chat about what it's like to work on the data team at ProPublica - which is currently hiring!
speakers: Hannah Fresques, ProPublica; Scott Klein; Simon Rogers, Google; Andrea Suozzo, ProPublica

title: Mastering NotebookLM for journalists
description: Transform your reporting workflow with NotebookLM, your personal AI research assistant. We will cover how to instantly synthesize complex beats, generate inline citations, and explore features like audio overviews, mind maps, and data extraction-all while keeping your materials organized and searchable.
speakers: Mago Torres, Google News Initiative

title: Spatial analysis in R: 301
description: Data that lacks a shared column for direct joins can often still be connected through maps. But common geographies like census tracts and precincts rarely align perfectly. Despite these challenges, it's still possible to estimate how votes shifted across neighborhoods or calculate statistical relationships using Census data. This process is called interpolation - learn how to translate data from one map to another and bridge geographic gaps in their data analysis. This session is good for journalists who are comfortable with the basics of R and Rstudio. Attending "Spatial analysis in R: 201" is recommended. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Ryan Little, ProPublica; Nick McMillan, ProPublica

title: A view from both sides: 5 practical strategies for successful public records requests
description: Employing a smart strategy from the outset can make all the difference between progress and pushback when it comes to accessing public records. This session offers insights from both the PIO and journalist perspectives to help you shape your requests to be as successful as possible and know where to turn when facing delays or denials.
speakers: Luke Britt, Marion County Superior Courts; Kris Cundiff, Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (RCFP); Casey Smith, Indiana Capital Chronicle

title: Building blocks of a better team
description: Is your team clicking together like a perfectly built structure or clunking along like mismatched pieces? Strong collaboration and communication are the foundation of any high-performing team, and in this interactive session, you'll discover how to strengthen both through the power of play. Using building blocks as both metaphor and hands-on tool, we'll explore what it takes to build trust, align around shared goals and adapt to change. You'll gain fresh insights into team dynamics, leadership styles and the invisible structures that support effective teamwork. Come ready to build, reflect and leave with practical strategies for assembling a more connected, resilient team: one brick at a time.
speakers: Tara Puckey, Radio Television Digital News Association

title: Use our quiz to focus on policy, not politics, in 2026
description: Help your audience prioritize public policy over political party or personality in the 2026 elections with QuizTheVote. It will transform your existing candidate questionnaires into an interactive quiz in about an hour. We'll talk about how to prepare for and implement QuizTheVote, an open source resource built on Google sheets with no coding required.
speakers: sandra fish, QuizTheVote; Evan Wyloge, Independent journalist

title: Stadium pulse: Building sports data visualizations on deadline
description: Want to create compelling sports graphics fast? In this hands-on class, you'll build a "How's your favorite college basketball team doing?" visualization using March Madness data. We'll use Python and Jupyter notebooks to pull live data from an API, then create charts in Datawrapper. You'll learn the workflow for going from raw data to published graphic quickly: automating data collection, choosing which stats tell the story, and designing for both casual fans and stats enthusiasts. An understanding of basics in Python is helpful, but not required, for this session. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Rahul Deshpande, Atlanta Journal-Constitution; Nick Devlin, ESPN

title: Google Sheets: Advanced pivot tables (repeat)
description: You've done a few pivot tables and are getting curious about what more you could do with them. What happens if you aggregate by more than one column? What are those "column" and "filter" boxes for? Come unlock the full potential of pivot tables in this intermediate spreadsheet class. This session is good for: People familiar with spreadsheets and aggregating data with pivot tables, or anyone who has taken Sheets 1-3. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) for the training and will need a free Google account to participate.
speakers: Catherine Allen, POLITICO

title: From vibes to scores: Build your own AI benchmark
description: You've built some sort of AI workflow - maybe a copy editor, idea generator, automated podcast, research assistant or tip pipeline - but how do you know how well it really works? And how do you know when it stops working? In this session participants will learn how to define "good enough" for an AI use case, implement both code-based and LLM-as-judge scorers, build a small test representative test set, and run evaluations. We'll be using Pydantic AI (Python) with the Braintrust platform, but concepts are perfectly portable to no-code tools or other evaluation toolkits. Participants should have a basic understanding of Python and have at least basic experience with AI use cases.
speakers: Jonathan Soma, Columbia University

title: NICAR-L hackathon 🤠
description: The NICAR-L listserv has long been a source of inspiration and knowledge-sharing for data journalists around the world. But tapping into the decades of wisdom contained in its archives can be difficult with existing tools. Let's build something better! This session is good for people who get excited about cleaning dirty, inconsistently formatted data and building user-friendly tools. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
speakers: Cody Winchester, Investigative Reporters & Editors

title: AI tools journalists can use ethically
description: Artificial intelligence is reshaping how media-related professionals work, make decisions, and tell stories. From content creation and audience analysis to workflow automation and risk management, AI is no longer theoretical- it is embedded in daily practice.This session focuses on how media professionals are using AI tools responsibly. Participants will explore real-world applications, understand limitations and risks, and discuss how to integrate AI without sacrificing clarity, credibility, or values.
speakers: Benét Wilson, IRE & NICAR

title: Introduction to using satellite data for investigations
description: Satellite imagery can be a potential game-changer for investigative journalists - if you know how to access, choose, and analyze the image for little to no cost. In this hands-on workshop, a journalist with deep knowledge of satellite providers will share insider tips on how to acquire medium and even high-resolution images from commercial and public sources, as well as how to filter and use these resources to give investigations into topics like the scars of war or environmental disasters much greater reach. This session is good for beginners. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Laura Kurtzberg, Investigative Reporters & Editors

title: Google Sheets: Importing & data prep (repeat)
description: Don't give up if your data isn't presented in a neat spreadsheet. This session will teach you how to get data into a spreadsheet and prepare it for analysis. We will look at how to import text files, deal with data in a PDF, and get a table on a web page into a spreadsheet. This session is good for: Anyone comfortable working in Google Sheets. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) for the training and will need a free Google account to participate.
speakers: Scott Pham, CBS News

title: Navigating federal campaign finance data
description: This class will focus on using the FEC's web tools to access data. It will feature a hands-on demonstration of accessing reports as soon as they are filed. Participants will learn what is included in the reports, how to interpret entries, and potential pitfalls. The presenter will share a user-friendly spreadsheet template for viewing downloaded report data and walk participants through a simple recipe for creating compelling stories using campaign finance data. This session is good for everyone. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Brendan Glavin, OpenSecrets

title: Data-driven stories ideas for students
description: Data-driven investigations don't have to be long projects. In this panel, journalism educators will break down student data stories you can replicate in your own classroom or community. We'll cover the data, the process and what can go wrong.
speakers: Diana Fuentes, Investigative Reporters & Editors; Gerry Lanosga, Indiana University; Craig Lyons, Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism

title: Quick-hit investigations
description: Learn strategies, story ideas, resources and tips for watchdog stories on a variety of beats. You'll get tips on how to produce meaty enterprise stories that take days or weeks rather than months.
speakers: Adam Rhodes, IRE & NICAR; Francisco Vara-Orta, Investigative Reporters & Editors

title: Taking it all home
description: You've picked up some new skills, filled your brain with inspiration and your notebook with ideas, but now what? We'll give you some tips on how to take advantage of what you've learned, avoid letting your skills get rusty and even continue learning when you get back to your job. Bring your questions, too! We'll have lots of time for that.
speakers: Leah Becerra, Reynolds Journalism Institute; Laine Cibulskis, University of Missouri; Liz Lucas, University of Missouri

title: Introduction to the command line (OSX) (repeat)
description: Too often in data journalism we forget about the basics. And it doesn't get as basic as the command line. Even knowing a little will make your job easier. We will run through some simple commands, dive into working with spreadsheets and show you some handy tools he frequently uses at work. This session is good for: People who feel intimidated by the command line on their computer, but want to explore the power of command line tools. Laptops will be provided.
speakers: Brent Jones, NPR

title: Today's News for Tomorrow Q&A
speakers: Laura Moscoso, Investigative Reporters & Editors

title: Kick more ass with a Python class
description: Tired of chasing global variables through a bajillion lines of linear code? Piling up dozens of functions that never seem to quite cohere into an efficient workflow? There's a better way! Discover how to model your data with custom Python classes, creating reusable blueprints that make your code easier to read and harder to break. This session is good for folks who are comfortable writing basic Python scripts, including custom functions, but are new to object-oriented programming.
